insights: All 500 incidents are assigned exclusively to a fixed team of five individuals, indicating a structural constraint—not an operational imbalance—in the assignment policy.

Hardware incidents constitute 81% of the total (405/500) and are distributed relatively evenly across the five assignees (17.0%–23.7%), confirming no individual-level skew within the dominant category.

Non-Hardware incidents (95 total) are also handled solely by the same five assignees, with shares ranging from 10.5% to 30.5%, reinforcing that workload distribution is consistent across categories despite volume disparity.

The apparent overall assignment concentration (top 4 assignees handling 83% of incidents) is an artifact of aggregating across categories—not evidence of inequitable allocation—since all assignments are bounded by the same five-person pool.

No missing or unassigned incidents exist, and no other assignees appear in the dataset, confirming that the 'imbalance' reflects intentional role consolidation rather than inconsistent routing, governance failure, or data quality issues.

The uniformity of assignee participation across both Hardware and non-Hardware groups eliminates category-specific bottlenecks or preferential routing as explanatory factors for distribution patterns.
summary: The user's goal was to identify discrepancies or imbalances in incident assignment, but the analysis reveals no inequitable distribution—only a tightly controlled, policy-driven assignment structure where all 500 incidents are handled exclusively by five designated individuals. Within the dominant Hardware group (81% of incidents), workload is evenly shared (17.0%–23.7% per assignee), and the same pattern holds for non-Hardware incidents, confirming consistency rather than skew. The observed concentration at the aggregate level stems entirely from this fixed-team constraint, not favoritism, routing errors, or systemic bias. This reflects intentional role consolidation, not a process failure—so the priority shifts from correcting imbalance to evaluating whether this five-person model remains operationally sustainable as incident volume or complexity evolves.
